paper_title: Palynological Study of Jasminum sambac Accessions

methods.description: Procedure for collecting Jasminum sambac accessions from various locations and fixing polliniferous material and anthers for subsequent palynological analysis.

steps.description: Twenty accessions of Jasminum sambac (L.) Ait. were collected from various locations in the State of Kerala.
steps.searchable_summary: Collect plant accessions from specified locations
inputs: Locations in the State of Kerala

steps.description: Polliniferous materials from the collected specimen were fixed in glacial acetic acid.
steps.searchable_summary: Collect polliniferous material from collected plant accessions

steps.description: Polliniferous materials from the collected specimen were fixed in glacial acetic acid.
steps.searchable_summary: Fix polliniferous material in glacial acetic acid

steps.description: Fresh anthers from mature unopened flower buds were fixed in glacial acetic acid.
steps.searchable_summary: Collect fresh anthers from mature unopened flower buds
inputs: Mature unopened flower buds from accessions

steps.description: Fresh anthers from mature unopened flower buds were fixed in glacial acetic acid.
steps.searchable_summary: Fix fresh anthers in glacial acetic acid

methods.description: Preparation of pollen slides from fixed anthers using the standardized acetolysis method for subsequent microscopic examination.

steps.description: Pollen preparations were made by the acetolysis method standardized by Erdtman (1952) and modified by Nair (1970).
steps.searchable_summary: Prepare pollen slides using the acetolysis method

methods.description: Detailed microscopic examination, measurement, and description of pollen morphological features from prepared slides, including shape, size, aperture types, and exine ornamentation.

steps.description: The pollen shape and size classes were ascertained following the classifications suggested by Erdtman(1966) and Walker & Doyle (1975) respectively.
steps.searchable_summary: Classify pollen shape and size based on standard classifications

steps.description: The terminologies suggested by Punt et al. (1994) were adopted for describing the aperture types and exine ornamentation patterns.
steps.searchable_summary: Adopt standard palynological terminologies for description

steps.description: Measurements relating to the following characters were taken using an ocular micrometer.
steps.searchable_summary: Measure pollen characters using an ocular micrometer

steps.description: In each case, the mean values of the measurements were taken from a random sample of 30 pollen grains.
steps.searchable_summary: Select a random sample of 30 pollen grains for measurement for each character
outputs: Selected random sample of 30 pollen grains per character

steps.description: In each case, the mean values of the measurements were taken from a random sample of 30 pollen grains.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate the mean values of measurements from the sampled pollen grains
inputs: Raw pollen measurements from 30 grains per character
outputs: Mean pollen measurements for each character

steps.description: The major qualitative palynological features were also conserved within the species with the members having mostly spheroidal, 3-zonocolporate, medium-sized pollen with reticulate exine ornamentation (Tables 3a-b). Only one accession (AC 5) showed occasionally 4-zonocolporate pollen in addition to the more frequent 3-zonocolporate ones. Although the lumina were irregular in size due to varying mural traits, the pollen could be broadly categorized into homobrochate and heterobrochate types (Figs. 1, 20). The former was observed in only three taxa (AC 2, 3 and 10). Muri were very tall, narrow or broad, with upper end flat and smooth in most accessions, except in AC 5 where it was pyramidal (Fig. 11). Small granular warts were observed on the mural surface in some taxa -AC 2, 3, 4, and 17. The accession AC 12 showed slight granulation on its mural surfaces, with the granules not assuming the form of warts. The accessions AC 8, 14 and 15 showed spiny projections from the mural wall (Plate III). The lumina were very deep with the luminal floor not clearly visible due to the tallness of the mural walls in almost all cases, except in AC 20 where few warts and perforations were noted in the lumina. Even in this case, the mural walls were tall. In homobrochate forms the lumina appeared oval or ellipsoidal, while the heterobrochate pollen had narrow and irregularly elongated lumina. Interestingly, the four taxa with pyramidal projection or spiny outgrowths from the mural wall had very short and irregularly narrow lumina giving a porous appearance to the pollen exine. In all other taxa, the lumina were more prominent giving a reticulate appearance. The lumina appeared to be plugged with waxy material in three accessions -AC 4, 5 and 15.
steps.searchable_summary: Observe and describe major qualitative palynological features
outputs: Qualitative pollen descriptions (e.g., shape, aperture type, exine pattern, lumina characteristics, muri characteristics, presence of warts/granules/spines, waxy material)

steps.description: Although the lumina were irregular in size due to varying mural traits, the pollen could be broadly categorized into homobrochate and heterobrochate types (Figs. 1, 20).
steps.searchable_summary: Categorize pollen into homobrochate and heterobrochate types based on lumina characteristics
